WCAG Issue Collection

8 issues · Exported March 9, 2026

#1 Non-interactive headings are focusable via keyboard tabbing ▼
High 2.1.1
keyboard and focus

This issue primarily relates to WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) because non-interactive headings should not be reachable using the keyboard to prevent confusion and maintain a logical focus sequence. Additionally, WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) is relevant since the keyboard focus should follow a meaningful order that typically skips non-interactive elements like headings. WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) may also apply by ensuring that headings are properly marked as non-interactive to help assistive technologies understand the page structure correctly.

WCAG Criteria

2.1.1 (A) 2.4.3 (A) 1.3.1 (A)

Issue Description

Non interactive headings can be reached with the TAB key

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Screenshots

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#2 Invisible interactive element receives keyboard focus on tabbing ▼
High 2.1.2
keyboard and focus

This issue primarily relates to WCAG 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap), because an invisible interactive element that receives keyboard focus can confuse users and interfere with smooth keyboard navigation. Additionally, WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) may be relevant since invisible elements often lack proper accessible names or roles, making them difficult for assistive technologies to interpret. Together, these criteria highlight problems with keyboard accessibility and the clarity of interactive elements.

WCAG Criteria

2.1.2 (A) 4.1.2 (A)

Issue Description

When first pressing the TAB key to navigate the page, there is an invisible interactive element that can be tabbed to.

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#3 Remove tabindex=0 from non-interactive divs in My Stats area ▼
High 2.1.2
keyboard and focus

This issue relates primarily to WCAG 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap) and 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) because non-interactive div elements are given tabindex=0, allowing them to receive keyboard focus without providing meaningful interaction or an accessible name and role. This can confuse keyboard users and assistive technologies, as the elements appear focusable but do not behave as expected. Additionally, WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) may be relevant since improper tabindex usage can disrupt the natural sequence of keyboard navigation.

WCAG Criteria

2.1.2 (A) 4.1.2 (A) 2.4.3 (A)

Issue Description

Non-interactive divs in the My Stats area has tabindex=0

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#4 Incorrect keyboard focus order prevents reaching filters after button ▼
Critical 2.4.3
keyboard and focus

This issue relates primarily to WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) because the keyboard navigation does not follow the visual layout, making it confusing for users to reach the filters in a logical sequence. Additionally, WCAG 3.2.1 (On Focus) may be involved since the focus changes cause unexpected shifts that interfere with the user’s ability to access elements as anticipated. Together, these criteria address the importance of intuitive and predictable keyboard navigation that aligns with what is visually presented.

WCAG Criteria

2.4.3 (A) 3.2.1 (A)

Issue Description

When activating the filter button using a keyboard, the user can not reach the filters unless they press Shift + Tab, which is not intuitive as the filters appear after the button on the screen.

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#5 Reduce tab key presses to navigate My Reqs filters efficiently ▼
High 2.4.3
keyboard and focus

This issue primarily relates to WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) because users must be able to navigate through interactive elements in a logical and efficient sequence using the keyboard. Requiring multiple tab key presses to move past a single filter disrupts this flow and creates unnecessary barriers. Additionally, WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) is relevant, as all functionality should be accessible with straightforward keyboard input without redundant key presses. WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible) may also apply since consistent and clear focus indicators support users in understanding their current position during keyboard navigation, which is hindered when navigation requires extra tabbing.

WCAG Criteria

2.4.3 (A) 2.1.1 (A) 2.4.7 (AA)

Issue Description

When My Reqs filters are open, each filter requires a minumum of 3 TAB KEY presses to navigate past it. These can be conslidated into one.

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#6 Dropdown menu in My Reqs filters not operable by keyboard ▼
Critical 2.1.1
keyboard and focus

This issue relates primarily to WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) and 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap) because the dropdown menu cannot be operated or exited using a keyboard alone, which is essential for users who rely on keyboard navigation. Additionally, WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) may be relevant if the dropdown lacks proper labels or roles that assistive technologies use to identify and interact with it. Together, these criteria ensure the dropdown is accessible and fully usable without a mouse.

WCAG Criteria

2.1.1 (A) 2.1.2 (A) 4.1.2 (A)

Issue Description

Dropdown menu in the My Reqs filters area can not be operated with a keyboard.

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#7 Invisible focusable element at top of My Reqs filters ▼
High 2.4.7
keyboard and focus

This issue relates primarily to WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible), WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard), and WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) because it involves an element that can receive keyboard focus but is invisible, making it difficult for users to know where the focus is and potentially causing confusion for assistive technologies. Additionally, the presence of invisible focusable elements may lead to keyboard traps (WCAG 2.1.2) or unexpected context changes when focused (WCAG 3.2.1), which can disrupt keyboard navigation and user understanding. Overall, this affects keyboard operability and focus clarity.

WCAG Criteria

2.4.7 (AA) 2.1.1 (A) 4.1.2 (A) 2.1.2 (A) 3.2.1 (A)

Issue Description

At the top of the My Reqs filters when opened, there is an invisible element that can receive focus.

Assistive Tech:
keyboad only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026

#8 Make sidebar AI icon menu item keyboard interactive ▼
High 2.1.1
keyboard and focus

This issue primarily relates to WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) because all interactive functionality must be operable using a keyboard, and having an element that can receive focus but is not interactive violates this requirement. Additionally, WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) is relevant since focusable elements should be arranged logically and be functional when focused, and WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) may apply if the element lacks appropriate roles or states that enable assistive technologies to understand its interactivity. Overall, the problem affects keyboard operability and the accurate identification of interactive controls.

WCAG Criteria

2.1.1 (A) 2.4.3 (A) 4.1.2 (A)

Issue Description

The menu item in the side bar (AI Icon) can be tabbed to, but is not interactive.

Assistive Tech:
keyboard only
Browser:
Chrome
Operating System:
macOS

Saved Mar 9, 2026
